content: It’s easy to play any musical instrument: all you have to do is touch the right key at the right time and the instrument will play itself. - Johann Sebastian Bach
I'm finally getting into Bach more seriously - just in time to give his music 10 or 20 years of study.

His Ricercar, and especially this version of it, makes it really clear how music is (or can be) a form of geometry:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?reload=9&v=KYouXtuk0T8
There are over 1000 compositions by Bach, and now it's time for me to get to know them all, or at least as many as I can.

The most catchy of the fifteen two-part Inventions, in my opinion, are:

  • Invention No.4 in D minor, BWV 775

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=O2jpnkEOH3A

• Invention No. 8 in F major, BWV 779

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=0ShOyEGGvkY

• Invention No. 14 in B-flat major, BWV 785

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=-FSslu4Itz8

I'm not saying they're the "best", just that when I listen to these I instantly say "oh, I've heard that".

(And I haven't tried to choose the best performances on YouTube, just ones that show the sheet music!)

John Baez said:

There are over 1000 compositions by Bach, and now it's time for me to get to know them all, or at least as many as I can.

Like basically everyone at the time Bach was a jobbing composer, and churned out a lot of stuff just to pay the rent... some people say even that stuff is genius, but I'm not personally convinced
In case you're interested in Bach's piano stuff, Glenn Gould probably can't be topped.

Jules Hedges said:

Like basically everyone at the time Bach was a jobbing composer, and churned out a lot of stuff just to pay the rent... some people say even that stuff is genius, but I'm not personally convinced

Well, it can't all be equally good, but I suspect even "bad" stuff by Bach is worth listening to.

Fawzi Hreiki said:

In case you're interested in Bach's piano stuff, Glenn Gould probably can't be topped.

I like his recordings of Bach, so I should listen to them all!

I get annoyed when I hear him muttering to himself.

I also like some other pianists, for example I'm enjoying Beatrice Rana's Goldberg Variations immensely. And I also like to hear Bach's keyboard pieces on harpischord. I only recently managed to get myself to enjoy the piano versions (again). The way the piano lets you separate parts using different dynamics, and the extra expressiveness, is definitely a plus. But there's something nice about hearing it more like the way it was composed. (I am not one of those who has an allergy to the sounds of the harpsichord.)
Itzhak Perlman's versions of the violin sonatas are also very very good.
The harpsichord is nice but it can sometimes be a bit maximalist and almost overwhelming.

I have Perlman's version of the violin sonatas, and have loved them for years.

The amount of Bach I actually own is small enough to list:

  • Izhtak Perlman, Sonatas and Partitas
  • Andras Schiff, Two-Part and Three-Part Inventions (piano)
  • Beatrice Rana, Goldberg Variations (piano)
  • Gustav Leonhart, The Well-Tempered Clavier 1 (harpsichord and cembalo)
  • Keith Jarrett, The Well-Tempered Clavier 2 (harpsichord)
  • Gustav Leonhart, Goldberg Variations (harpsichord)
  • Yo-Yo Ma, 6 Suites for Unaccompanied Cello
  • Juilliard String Quartet, The Art of Fugue
  • Concerto Alessandrini, The Art of the Fugue and Keyboard Concertos (harpsichord, flute and violin)
  • Paul Galbraith, Lute Suites (8-string guitar)
Gould's Partitas and Concertos are good. Also, Harnoncourt's St. Matthew Passion.
Mass in B Minor by Philippe Herreweghe.
Thanks!
For guitar, Milan Zelenka's album is pretty good. As is Kiyoshi Shomura.

I recently read that Bach's "lute suites" were not composed for lute:

  • Clive Titmuss, The myth of Bach’s lute suites.

They sure sound good on an 8-string guitar!
In fact a whole bunch of Bach sounds very warm and friendly on guitar.
I think primarily because the couterpoint works very well on classical guitar.
Also, if you're getting into Baroque music more broadly, the soundtrack for Kubrick's Barry Lyndon is a goldmine.
And of course the film itself is too.

Fawzi Hreiki said:

I think primarily because the couterpoint works very well on classical guitar.

It does (though I don't know how anyone can play such fancy stuff on the guitar). But what I really like is how Bach sounds more "intimate", less "cold" on guitar than on piano.
bach solo violin pieces played on the mandolin (especially by the genius that is chris thile) are absolutely unbeatable to me
the first piece of bach that actually made me like bach (and not hate the organ!) was his “little fugue in g minor”
Played on the organ?

I've always liked Bach... my parents would often play the Modern Jazz Quartet's Blues on Bach as a kid, which I still love, but also actual straight Bach, which I don't remember so distinctly.

(Oh yeah: my parents also had Wendy Carlos' Switched-On Bach, on the Moog, which seemed pretty cool back when it came out back in 1968. It hasn't aged as well as Blues on Bach.)

But when I got really into music as angst-ridden teenager, I was more into rock, modern classical, electronic and other kinds of music. It ain't that I didn't like Bach: his music always seemed perfect in its own way; it just didn't give me what I needed most back then.

Now that I'm happier and older, it seems worth really digging into Bach, trying to figure out how his counterpoint works...

John Baez said:

His Ricercar, and especially this version of it, makes it really clear how music is (or can be) a form of geometry:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?reload=9&v=KYouXtuk0T8

I remember Eugenia Cheng giving a talk on a piece of Bach's representing a braid in the way the parts crossed over one another

Here's one of my favorites, pianist Alfred Brendel playing the Italian Concerto and Chromatic Fantasia:

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=1piyPMkEK9A&list=OLAK5uy_lWtaZIq7BNEHkgjvIMLneGbZWkGvkjOS0

Here's a nice guitar version of Chromatic Fantasia by Christophe Dejour

https://www.youtube.com/watch?v=ug4_-EvG_-Y

John Baez said:

In fact a whole bunch of Bach sounds very warm and friendly on guitar.

I second this. I've been learning a few pieces by Bach on guitar and it sounds really nice. I've learned Bourree and Air on a G String and now in the process of learning BWV1007 Prelude (the famous cello piece). Especially Air sounds like it was made to be played on guitar.
if you don’t mind a bit of “sacrilege”, then jacques loussier has some brilliant jazz bach covers
I was about to suggest Jacques Loussier, but then forgot about it. Thanks for reminding me, Tim! To deepen the sacrilege, Loussier also did some nice interpretations of Chopin's Nocturnes.
Oh, and apparently they also played Sati's Gnossienne and some pieces by Georg Friedrich Händel.
I have Jacques Loussier's Satie album and have enjoyed it. Thanks for the selection, @Refurio Anachro! I'll play them after my wife wakes up.

Here's a nice story of a musical threat:

Hans von Bülow called The Well-Tempered Clavier the "Old Testament" of music (the Beethoven Sonatas were the "New Testament"). In the liner notes to the Clair de Lune compilation of piano encores issued by CBS Masterworks, Philippe Entremont relates an anecdote in which von Bülow, having a distaste for the endless clamor for encores, was facing a thunderously applauding house and raised his hand, saying "Ladies and Gentlemen! If you do not stop this immediately I shall play you Bach's 48 preludes and fugues from beginning to end!" The audience laughed but also stopped applauding as they knew von Bülow was able to perform the work from memory.
Interestingly, the logician and puzzle creator Raymond Smullyan has recorded some pieces from The Well-Tempered Clavier which are available on Wikipedia (see note 58)

Woo-hoo! I just bought an mp3 version of Gustav Leonhardt Plays Bach for $11.99. It's a 10-CD set containing:

  • The Well-Tempered Clavier (2 CDs)
  • 24 Preludes and Fugures (2 CDs)
  • The Art of the Fugue
  • Overture In The French Manner, Italian Concerto in F Major
  • Prelude, Fugue & Allegro in E Flat Major
  • Organ Works, Chorale Arrangements Of The Third Part of "Clavier-Übung", Prelude and Fugue in E Minor
  • Sonatas For Violin And Harpsichord
  • Goldberg Variations
This should keep me going for a while...

Yes, there's so much I need to check out now! I enjoyed Chris Thile's and the stuff from Modern Jazz Quartet, both of which were completely new to me! Chris has a good hand, even though I find his instrument a bit crude. And some of that MJQ is so far out that I'll need binoculars to see where Johan Sebastian is hiding in there. I also enjoyed Bach's inventiones very much, these seemed somewhat familiar. Thanks everybody for these exquisite links!
I have a bunch of recordings from an organist, but I find them hard to enjoy. One day I might try to snatch some fugues as midi files and see if I can help myself into them.
I'm less into organ music than harpsichord or piano. In Mozart's day Bach was mainly known as a composer of organ music...

Obligatory mention of Douglas Hofstadter’s “Gödel, Escher, and Bach: an Eternal Golden Braid”, which features dialogues in the style of Lewis Carroll’s “What the Tortoise said to Achilles” but using forms adapted from the music of Bach :) plus lots of philosophical discussion in between.

(I apparently talked my advisor into starting reading it over break :D)

Personally I have started learning his sonatas and partitas (which were written for the violin) on my cello. I’ve always been envious of the beauty of these works so I decided I had enough technique to steal them and play them myself (in their original keys).

Maybe a little tangential, but I’ve always felt like the form/structure/shape of music (or anything bound to a linear domain like time or plain words on page) is the hardest part to innovate – it’s bound to resemble some existing pattern that’s been explored before.
Maybe it helps to think of music as higher-dimensional. There's certainly a nice 2-dimensional aspect, as shown in this movie:
https://www.youtube.com/watch?reload=9&v=KYouXtuk0T8
The opening of Chopin's E minor prelude is an exploration of edges paths through a 4-dimensional cube whose vertices are seventh chords.
In general, thinking about music in new ways is a good way to generate new forms.

Someone gave categorical semantics to Generalized Musical Intervals and Transformations (GMIT). I've got Lewin's book around somewhere, but I'm far cry in terms of music theory to be able to truly follow it. The idea is nevertheless compelling.

Alexandre Popoff, Moreno Andreatta and Andree Ehresmann wrote a nice paper using category theory

  • Relational PK-Nets for Transformational Music Analysis.

I talked about with them so I know it makes sense!
